titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Lynn Nottage Papers
titlestmt.author: by Elise Riley

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This collection contains 1 published digital object.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday February 6, 2025 at 23:02

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The Lynn Nottage papers consist of correspondence, writings, production files, journals, family papers, printed material, photographs, electronic files, and other papers documenting Lynn Nottage's (Yale Drama 1989) personal and professional life.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Boxes 13, 14, and 62 (research and production files): Restricted. Not to be consulted before January 1, 2034.
Box 55 (audiovisual material): Restricted fragile. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 56 (born digital): Restricted fragile. Access copies of digital files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 60 (financial, student and personal records): Restricted until 2080.
Email: A portion of email is restricted and not to be consulted before January 1, 2034.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Granary Books, Inc. on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, Theatre Guild Fund, Sinclair Lewis Fund, and the Adele Gutman Nathan Theatrical Collection Fund, 2023.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This collection is arranged in eight series: I. Correspondence, 1972-2022. II. Drafts, 1981-2023. III. Research and production files, 1979-2022. IV. Professional papers, 1972-2023. V. Family papers, 1942-2021. VI. Works by others, 1981-2022. VII. Audiovisual materials, 1968-2004. VIII. Computer media, 1997-2022.

bioghist.head: Lynn Nottage (1964-)
bioghist.p: Lynn Nottage (1964-) is an award-winning Black American playwright and screenwriter. She is the first woman to receive the Pulitzer Prize for Drama twice. Her plays, which have been produced in the United States and abroad, center the working-class and the under-represented. She has won two Obie awards, a Tony award, a MacArthur Fellowship, and a Guggenheim Fellowship, among many other honors.

Nottage was born in Brooklyn, New York. She was raised by her mother Ruby, a schoolteacher and principal, and her father Wallace, a child psychologist. Ruby and Wallace Nottage participated in local politics, activism, art, and community. Her parents were involved in projects including Harlem Youth Opportunities Unlimited, Freedom Schools during the Civil Rights Movement, the Brooklyn YWCA, the Martin Luther Commission, and the Coalitions for Black Parents. Nottage attended the Fiorello H. LaGuardia High School of Music & Art and completed her undergraduate degree at Brown University in 1986, followed by her MFA in Drama at Yale University in 1989. She worked as a press officer at Amnesty International for four years before becoming a playwright full-time.

In high school Nottage wrote her first play, The Dark Side of Verona , which won her a spot in a writing workshop lead by Stephen Sondheim. Her first staged production, entitled Rhinestones and Paste , was put on at Yale University in 1988. Her next play, Poof! , was presented at the Actors Theatre of Louisville in 1993 during the Humana Festival of New American Plays. Por'Knockers premiered off-Broadway at the Vineyard Theatre in 1995. Nottage premiered two plays in 1996, Crumbs from the Table of Joy at South Coast Repertory and Mud, River, Stone at The Acting Company.

During the 21st century, Nottage's plays reached wider audiences and she began winning grants and prestigious awards. Her works from this period included: Las Meninas (2002), Intimate Apparel (2003), Fabulation, Or the Re-Education of Udine (2004), Ruined (2007), By the Way, Meet Vera Stark (2011), Sweat (2015), Mlima's Tale (2018), and Floyd's (later renamed, Clyde's , 2019).

The production of Intimate Apparel in 2003 led to the biggest shift in Nottage's career thus far. It was her first collaboration with director Kate Whoriskey, who would become a long-time collaborator. Whoriskey directed many of Nottage's plays including Ruined and Sweat for which Nottage won a Pulitzer Prize each. She wrote the book for the stage production of The Secret Life of Bees in 2019. Nottage won a Tony for Best Book of a Musical for MJ the Musical in 2022.
In addition to playwriting, Nottage writes and produces for television, film, and opera. She teaches at Columbia University.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Lynn Nottage Papers. James Weldon Johnson Memorial Collection in the Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Lynn Nottage papers includes correspondence, drafts, production binders, notebooks, family papers, print material, photographs, electronic files, and ephemera by or relating to Lynn Nottage's (Yale Drama 1989) personal and professional life. These papers document Nottage's career as a playwright and screenwriter, her creative and research processes, professional relationships with other playwrights and creators in the entertainment industry, as well as her personal life and family history. The bulk of the materials date from the 1980s to present.

subject.part: African Americans in the motion picture industry

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged chronologically by the materials' date of creation.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains correspondence to, by, or related to Lynn Nottage.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains drafts of plays, screenplays, and other works by Lynn Nottage. Some drafts include research and press related to that work.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this series are arranged alphabetically by title of work.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains research files, production binders, and notebooks generated by Lynn Nottage during her creative process. Nottage incorporates historically accurate details, sometimes from her family's history, in her plays and relies on extensive research for authenticity. The production binders may include drafts and press related to the work.

scopecontent.p: This series contains photographs, ephemera, awards, and publicity related to Lynn Nottage's professional career. Digital photographs can be found in the Computer Media series.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this series are arranged alphabetically by folder title.

bioghist.head: Ruby and Wallace Nottage
bioghist.p: Ruby and Wallace Nottage were the parents of playwright Lynn Nottage--they were significant figures in the communities in which Nottage came of age. Their political, artistic, and educational activism had an early influence on Nottage's own worldviews.

Ruby Nottage, after three decades of service in public schools, was elected Democractic District Leader of the 57th Assembly District. She also served on the Martin Luther King Commission, the board of the Brooklyn YMCA, and the Community Advisory Board for Medgar Evers College.

Wallace Nottage was a child psychologist, who later became director of Youth House and its Short Term Adolescent Resident Training program. As a former Harlem gang member who was expelled from high school, he became a powerful and relatablxe force for community education. Mr. Nottage was also notably involved with Kenneth Clark's HarYou program.
Both parents were involved in the Coalition for Black Parents and other grassroots educational initiatives that stemmed from the Civil Rights Movement.

The Nottages were friends with a wide range of people, especially artists. Their friend circle included Norman Lewis, Ernest Crichlow, and Romare Bearden, all of whom are represented in the Nottage's art collection. Part of this art collection was auctioned at Sotheby's and displayed at the Brooklyn YMCA. The Nottages' friends also included Richard Trent--the first president of Medgar Evers College--whose inscribed photo the Nottages kept; and actress/activist Vinnie Burrows.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains correspondence, records, photographs, and ephemera related to Lynn Nottage's family members, most notably her parents, Ruby and Wallace Nottage, and her grandmother, Dorothy Nottage. Also included is a personal collection of playbills compiled by Lynn Nottage and her mother-in-law, Edith Gerber.

did.unittitle: Dorothy Nottage certificate - National Council of Senior Citizens, inc.
The Edith Gerber and Gerber/Nottage collection of playbills

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lynn Nottage, her husband Tony Gerber, and her mother-in-law, Edith Gerber assembled this collection of playbills from Broadway productions dating back to 1942.

Notably present in this collection are playbills for works by playwrights including Eugene O'Neill, Jean Cocteau, William Carlos Williams, Albert Camus, Anton Chekhov, and Tennessee Williams. Productions span the gamut, from well-known productions such as Rent , Guys and Dolls , Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf , H.M.S. Pinafore , to smaller local productions by organizations like the Dramatic Workshop of the New School.

Many playbills have been inscribed with the date of attendance by Edith Gerber. Of particular interest are earlier works like Othello from the Sam S. Shubert Theatre (November 1943), as well as a performance by Marian Anderson at Brooklyn Academy of Music (1959). There are also several playbills from the 1990s, including productions at Yale University and smaller venues in New York City and Brooklyn.

did.unittitle: Richard and Cynthia to Wally and Ruby, photograph

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 60 (financial, student and personal records): Restricted until 2080.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains works by others, primarily printed typescripts of play drafts from others in Lynn Nottage's playwriting community. Of particular note in this series are works by Spike Lee, including a 1984 draft of She's Gotta Have It , a copy of the holograph screenplay for Lee's first film, as well as a thirty-page photocopied typescript for Joe's Bed-Stuy Barbershop: We Cut Heads (1981), a short film proposed for Lee's thesis project at New York University.

Also included is an early work by Kenneth Lonergan, whose Young Playwrights Festival contribution, By Far, the Heaviest Thing of All Time , was written when he was a teenager. Other writers whose works are included are Ayad Akhtar, Annie Baker, David Lindsay-Abaire, Frowin Paul Nyoni, Herman Wouk, Jim Lewis and Bill T. Jones, John Guare, Jonathan Lethem, Kia Corthron, Kristoffer Diaz, Lars von Trier, Lee Breuer, Martin McDonagh, Naomi Iizuka, Ruby Aiyo Gerber, Tony Gerber, Stew Rodewald, Jessica Blank, April Yvette Thompson, Lonnie Carter, Loy Arcenas, Marshall Brickman, and Rick Elice. Some materials are inscribed to Nottage, or were otherwise retained because of Nottage's relationship with the playwright.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains digital materials relating to Lynn Nottage's life and work, including email correspondence and photographs.
Email correspondence can be found in the Correspondence series.
